title: Venice Biennale Jury Excludes Russia and Israel from Awards Over ICC Charges
summary: In a bold move that has sent shockwaves through the art world, the all-women jury for the 61st Venice Biennale has declared it will not consider countries whose leaders are charged with crimes against humanity by the International Criminal Court (ICC) for its prestigious Golden Lion awards. While the jury's statement, published on April 23, does not name specific nations, the implications are clear: Russia and Israel, whose presidents Vladimir Putin and Benjamin Netanyahu face ICC arrest warrants for war crimes in Ukraine and Gaza respectively, are effectively excluded from contention. The jury, chaired by Solange Farkas and including Elvira Dyangani Ose, Zoe Butt, Marta Kuzma, and Giovanna Zapperi, stated that they "acknowledge the complex relationship between artist practice and nation-state representation" and do not want to bind artists' work with the actions of their governments. This decision comes as the Biennale Foundation faces intense criticism for allowing Russia to return after two consecutive absences following its invasion of Ukraine, and for securing a temporary pavilion for Israel despite ongoing protests. Nearly 10,000 people have signed an open letter opposing Russia's participation, and the European Union has threatened to suspend its €2 million (~$2.3 million) grant. Artists in the central exhibition, "In Minor Keys," wholeheartedly support the jury's decision. However, the Art Not Genocide Alliance (ANGA) welcomed the move but criticized the jury for not naming Israel directly, saying "such ambiguity sustains impunity." The jury invoked the late Artistic Director Koyo Kouoh's curatorial statement: "In refusing the spectacle of horror, the time has come to listen to the minor keys."
